title: Präpositionen + Akkusativ
explainer_md:
Five common prepositions **always** take the Akkusativ — no exceptions, no thinking needed: **[[durch]]** (through), **[[für]]** (for), **[[gegen]]** (against), **[[ohne]]** (without), **[[um]]** (around/at).

Memory trick: **d**urch, **f**ür, **g**egen, **o**hne, **u**m — say them as a rhythm a few times and they stick.

| Präposition | Beispiel | English |
|---|---|---|
| durch | durch **den** [[Park]] | through the park |
| für | für **meinen** Bruder | for my brother |
| gegen | gegen **die** Wand | against the wall |
| ohne | ohne **einen** Plan | without a plan |
| um | um **den** Tisch | around the table |

*Wir gehen durch **den** Park.* — We walk through the park.

*Das Geschenk ist für **dich**.* — The present is for you. (du → dich)

*Er spielt gegen **seinen** Freund.* — He plays against his friend.

*Ohne **meine** [[Brille]] sehe ich nichts.* — Without my glasses I see nothing.

**Tip:** after these five, the case is decided by the preposition alone — the verb doesn't matter anymore. So whenever you see *für*, the next article is Akkusativ, full stop.

payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Park).
„[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, but neuter das stays das (das Kind).
„[[gegen]]“ always takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die (die Wand).
„[[ohne]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen (der Bruder).
„[[um]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Tisch).
„[[um]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die (die Ecke).
„[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die (die Lehrerin).

payload.prompt: Ohne ___ Auto fahren wir mit dem Bus.
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ; neuter das stays das (das Auto).
„[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, also with pronouns: er → ihn.
„[[ohne]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, also with pronouns: du → dich.
„[[durch]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.

payload.prompt: Ich backe einen Kuchen für ___ Mutter. (die)
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine die stays die.
„[[um]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.
„[[gegen]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den.

payload.prompt: Er geht ohne ___ Jacke nach Hause. (seine)
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ, but feminine seine stays seine (die Jacke).

payload.prompt: Sie kauft ein Geschenk für ___ Bruder. (ihr)
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: ihr → ihren (der Bruder).

payload.translation: We are walking through the park.
payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ takes the Akkusativ: der → den. The verb stands in second position.
„[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, also with pronouns: du → dich.

payload.translation: He is coming without his brother.
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: sein → seinen (der Bruder).

payload.translation: The bus is driving around the corner.
payload.explanation: „[[um]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die. Der Bus is the Nominativ subject.

payload.translation: I am buying a book for him.
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ: er → ihn. The für-phrase can stand before or after the object.

reasons: It follows „durch“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows a dative preposition
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ is an Akkusativ preposition, so der Park becomes den Park.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „für“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ is an Akkusativ preposition; neuter das stays das, so the case is invisible but still Akkusativ.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows „ohne“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ is an Akkusativ preposition. Masculine: sein → seinen (der Bruder).

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „gegen“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: Der Ball is the one flying → subject → Nominativ. Only „die Wand“ follows the Akkusativ preposition „[[gegen]]“.

payload.prompt: Wir kaufen einen Kuchen für ___ Party.
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ + Akkusativ — feminine die stays die (die Party).
„[[durch]]“ + Akkusativ. Masculine: der Tunnel → den Tunnel.
„[[gegen]]“ + Akkusativ. Masculine sein → seinen.

payload.prompt: Sie joggt jeden Morgen durch ___ Wald. (der)
payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ + Akkusativ: der Wald → den Wald.
„[[für]]“ + Akkusativ — feminine meine doesn't change.
„[[um]]“ + Akkusativ: der See → den See.

payload.translation: Without my glasses I see nothing.
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ + Akkusativ. After the fronted phrase the verb stays in position 2: sehe ich.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „für“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ — feminine ihre stays ihre.
„[[gegen]]“ always takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die (die Tür).
„[[für]]“ always takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Vater).
„[[durch]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine die stays die (die Stadt).
„[[gegen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Plan).
„[[ohne]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, also with pronouns: ihr → euch.

payload.prompt: Wir machen das Essen für ___ Gäste. (unsere)
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ; plural unsere stays unsere — no Dativ -n here (die Gäste).
„[[durch]]“ takes the Akkusativ; neuter das stays das.

payload.prompt: Sie schreibt eine Karte für ___ Opa. (ihr)
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: ihr → ihren (der Opa).

payload.prompt: Ohne ___ Handy gehe ich nicht aus dem Haus. (mein)
payload.explanation: „[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ, but neuter mein stays mein (das Handy).
„[[gegen]]“ always takes the Akkusativ, also with pronouns: er → ihn.

payload.translation: The cat runs around the tree.
payload.explanation: „[[um]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: der → den (der Baum).

payload.translation: The ball flies through the window.
payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ takes the Akkusativ; neuter das stays das. Der Ball is the Nominativ subject.

payload.translation: I study every day for the exam.
payload.explanation: „[[für]]“ takes the Akkusativ. After the fronted für-phrase the verb stays in position 2: lerne ich.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „durch“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It follows a dative preposition
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: „[[durch]]“ is an Akkusativ preposition; feminine die stays die, so the case is invisible but still Akkusativ.

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „um“, which always takes the Akkusativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: Das Mädchen is the one running → subject → Nominativ. Only „den Tisch“ follows the Akkusativ preposition „[[um]]“.
